questions.a: ado-aw is a compiler that transforms natural-language markdown files with YAML front matter into Azure DevOps pipeline definitions. It enables AI agents to work safely in network-isolated sandboxes.
Build from source with `cargo build` or install with `cargo install --path .` from the repository root.

questions.q: How do I compile an agent file?
questions.a: Run `ado-aw compile ./path/to/agent.md` to compile a single file, or `ado-aw compile` to auto-discover and recompile all agentic workflows in the current directory.

questions.q: What is the three-stage pipeline model?
questions.a: Every compiled pipeline runs three sequential jobs: (1) Agent stage in a network-isolated sandbox producing safe-output proposals, (2) Detection stage inspecting for prompt injection/secret leaks, (3) Execution stage applying approved outputs with a write-capable token.

Safe outputs are the controlled set of actions an agent can propose (create PRs, comment on work items, etc.). They are validated in Stage 2 and executed in Stage 3, ensuring the agent never has direct write access.
Four targets: 'standalone' (default, full pipeline), '1es' (1ES Pipeline Template), 'job' (job-level ADO template), and 'stage' (stage-level ADO template).

Pipelines run in OneBranch's AWF (Agentic Workflow Firewall) network-isolated environment. Only explicitly allowed domains are accessible. Ecosystem domains (PyPI, npm, crates.io, etc.) can be enabled via the runtimes configuration.

Built-in MCP tools include GitHub integration (always-on), SafeOutputs (always-on), and configurable tools like azure-devops and cache-memory. Custom MCP servers can be added via stdio containers or HTTP backends.

questions.q: How do I configure an AI engine?
questions.a: Set the `engine:` field in front matter. Use the string form (`engine: copilot`) for defaults, or the object form to specify model, timeout-minutes, version, api-target, args, env, or command.

questions.q: How do I add runtime dependencies?
questions.a: Use the `runtimes:` field in front matter to declare Python, Node.js, .NET, or Lean runtimes. Each runtime supports version pinning, package installation, and authentication to private registries.
